metadata.title: Hawking Radiation and Black Hole Thermodynamics
metadata.description: Comprehensive database of Hawking radiation physics, black hole thermodynamics, entropy, evaporation, and the information paradox

hawkingTemperature.inverseMassRelation: T_H ∝ 1/M — smaller black holes are hotter

valuesForDifferentMasses.note: Hawking temp comparable to LHC energy scales
Evaporating now if formed in Big Bang (lifetime ~ age of universe)
60 nanokelvin — far below CMB temperature of 2.725K

comparisonWithCMB.note: Only BHs lighter than ~0.02 lunar masses are hotter than the CMB. All astrophysical BHs are colder than the CMB and actually ABSORB more radiation than they emit.

hawkingLuminosity.inverseMassSquaredRelation: L_H ∝ 1/M² — smaller BHs radiate enormously faster

valuesForDifferentMasses.note: ~400 GW — comparable to world power consumption

evaporationTime.cubicMassRelation: t_evap ∝ M³ — massive BHs last enormously longer

valuesForDifferentMasses.note: BHs with initial mass ~5×10¹¹ kg would be evaporating RIGHT NOW
10⁵⁷ times the age of the universe

primordialBHs.description: Black holes formed in the early universe could have masses where they are evaporating today
primordialBHs.criticalMass_note: A primordial BH with initial mass ~5×10¹¹ kg would evaporate now, potentially observable as gamma ray bursts
primordialBHs.observationalSearches: Fermi-LAT and other gamma-ray telescopes have searched for Hawking radiation from primordial BH evaporation — no confirmed detection

blackbodySpectrum.description: Hawking radiation has a blackbody spectrum modified by greybody factors

where.Gamma_omega: Greybody factor (transmission probability through the gravitational potential barrier)

emittedSpecies.description: A black hole emits all species of particles with mass m < kT_H/c²
forStellarBH_60nK: photons only (all other particles too massive)
emittedSpecies.note: At the very end of evaporation, the BH becomes hot enough to emit all known particles

greybodyFactors.description: The gravitational potential barrier around the BH partially reflects outgoing Hawking quanta, reducing the emission rate
greybodyFactors.lowFrequency_s0: Γ ∝ (ωRs)^(2l+2) for angular momentum mode l (geometric optics: only high-energy quanta escape efficiently)
greybodyFactors.highFrequency: Γ → 1 (geometric optics limit — all high-energy quanta escape)
greybodyFactors.effectOnSpectrum: Greybody factors suppress low-energy emission, making the actual spectrum slightly harder than a pure blackbody

unruhEffect.description: An observer uniformly accelerating through vacuum perceives thermal radiation at temperature T_U
unruhEffect.connectionToHawking: A static observer hovering at the horizon requires acceleration a = c⁴/(4GM) = surface gravity κ, and the Unruh temperature at this acceleration equals the Hawking temperature

surfaceGravity.kerr: κ = (r_+ - r_-)c² / (2(r_+² + a²)) where r_± = M ± √(M² - a²)

accelerationForKelvin.note: T_U = 1K requires acceleration of ~2.5 × 10²⁰ m/s² — extremely difficult to observe in the lab

virtualParticlePicture.description: Heuristic picture of Hawking radiation as virtual particle pair creation near the horizon
mechanism: Vacuum fluctuations constantly create virtual particle-antiparticle pairs everywhere
Near the horizon, the tidal gravitational field can separate the pair before they annihilate
One particle falls into the BH (with effectively negative energy as measured from outside)
The other escapes as real Hawking radiation
The BH mass decreases by the energy of the escaped particle
caveats: This picture is heuristic — the actual quantum field theory calculation does not require 'virtual particles'
The real calculation uses Bogoliubov transformations between in and out vacuum states
The emitted particles are NOT pair-produced — they emerge with a thermal spectrum
The 'negative energy partner' is not a real particle — it's a mathematical description of the BH losing mass

blackHoleThermodynamics.description: The four laws of black hole thermodynamics, paralleling the laws of ordinary thermodynamics

zerothLaw.statement: The surface gravity κ is constant over the event horizon of a stationary black hole
zerothLaw.thermodynamicAnalog: Temperature is constant throughout a body in thermal equilibrium

firstLaw.statement: dM = (κ/(8πG)) dA + Ω_H dJ + Φ_H dQ

secondLaw.classicalStatement: The area of the event horizon never decreases: dA ≥ 0 (Hawking area theorem, 1971)
secondLaw.generalizedSecondLaw: The generalized entropy S_gen = S_BH + S_outside never decreases
secondLaw.hawkingRadationException: Hawking radiation allows the area to decrease, but the entropy of the radiation more than compensates, so the generalized second law holds

thirdLaw.statement: It is impossible to reduce the surface gravity κ to zero by any finite sequence of operations
thirdLaw.thermodynamicAnalog: It is impossible to reach absolute zero temperature
thirdLaw.implication: An extremal Kerr BH (κ=0) cannot be achieved in practice
thirdLaw.note: The third law is weaker than in ordinary thermodynamics — there are gedanken experiments that approach κ=0 arbitrarily closely

fourthLaw.statement: The area of the event horizon can decrease via quantum effects (Hawking radiation), but the generalized entropy increases
fourthLaw.note: Sometimes not listed as a separate law, but it extends the framework to include quantum effects

entropyPerSolarMass.note: S_BH ∝ M² — entropy scales as mass squared

holographicPrinciple.description: The BH entropy is proportional to the AREA (not volume) of the event horizon
holographicPrinciple.implication: This suggests that the information content of any region is bounded by its surface area, not its volume — a key insight for quantum gravity

comparison.description: BH entropy is enormously larger than ordinary matter entropy
comparison.note: The entropy of supermassive black holes dominates the entropy budget of the observable universe

informationParadox.description: Hawking's original calculation implies that information falling into a black hole is destroyed when the BH evaporates, violating quantum mechanics (unitarity)

hawkingOriginal1976.claim: Black hole evaporation is not unitary — pure states evolve to mixed states
hawkingOriginal1976.implication: Information is permanently destroyed, violating a fundamental principle of quantum mechanics

pageTime.description: The Page time is when the BH has emitted half its entropy in Hawking radiation
pageTime.significance: If information is preserved, the entanglement entropy of the radiation should start decreasing after the Page time (Page curve)

pageCurve.description: The entanglement entropy of the Hawking radiation as a function of time
pageCurve.earlyTime: Entropy increases linearly (radiation is thermal, increasingly entangled with BH interior)
pageCurve.pageTime: Entropy reaches maximum (~half the BH entropy)
pageCurve.lateTime: Entropy decreases back to zero (information comes out — radiation becomes pure state)
pageCurve.shape: Inverted V — rises linearly, peaks at Page time, decreases to zero

proposedResolutions.name: Information comes out in radiation (Page)
proposedResolutions.description: Subtle correlations in the Hawking radiation encode the infalling information
proposedResolutions.status: Currently the mainstream view, supported by recent calculations

proposedResolutions.name: Black hole remnants
proposedResolutions.description: Evaporation stops at the Planck scale, leaving a remnant containing all the information

proposedResolutions.name: Baby universe
proposedResolutions.description: Information goes into a baby universe connected to ours only through the BH interior

proposedResolutions.name: Complementarity (Susskind, 1993)
proposedResolutions.description: Information is both inside and outside the BH, but no single observer can see both copies

proposedResolutions.name: Firewall (AMPS, 2012)
proposedResolutions.description: A firewall (high-energy curtain) at the horizon destroys infalling matter, resolving the paradox but violating the equivalence principle

proposedResolutions.name: ER = EPR (Maldacena & Susskind, 2013)
proposedResolutions.description: Einstein-Rosen bridges (wormholes) and Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen entanglement are the same phenomenon

proposedResolutions.name: Island rule (2019-2020)
proposedResolutions.description: Quantum extremal surface calculations in gravity show that 'islands' inside the BH contribute to the radiation entropy, reproducing the Page curve
proposedResolutions.status: Major breakthrough — widely considered the leading resolution
proposedResolutions.source: Penington, JHEP 2020, 2; Almheiri et al., JHEP 2020, 13

hawkingConcession.description: Hawking conceded that information IS preserved and the radiation IS unitary

phases.description: BH slowly evaporates, emitting low-energy quanta
phases.note: Most of the lifetime is in this phase

phases.description: As the BH shrinks, it gets hotter and evaporates faster (runaway process)
phases.note: T ∝ 1/M and L ∝ 1/M² and dM/dt ∝ -1/M², so M³ decreases linearly in time

phases.description: In the last fraction of a second, the BH releases its remaining mass-energy in a burst

energyReleased.note: Energy of about 5,000 one-megaton nuclear weapons released in 1 second

phases.description: When the BH reaches the Planck mass (~2.2×10⁻⁸ kg), quantum gravity effects become important and our current theories break down

valuesForDifferentSpins.note: Extremal Kerr has zero Hawking temperature

superradiantEmission.description: A Kerr BH preferentially emits particles co-rotating with the BH (superradiant modes)
superradiantEmission.spinDown: Hawking radiation carries away angular momentum, causing the BH to spin down
superradiantEmission.finalState: A Kerr BH evaporates to Schwarzschild (a→0) before its final explosion

analogGravityExperiments.description: Laboratory analogs of Hawking radiation using other physical systems

experiments.analogHorizon: Point where flow velocity exceeds speed of sound
experiments.result: Thermal spectrum consistent with Hawking prediction
experiments.source: Muñoz de Nova et al., Nature 569, 688 (2019)

experiments.analogHorizon: Hydraulic jump (where water flow exceeds wave speed)

experiments.medium: Optical fiber with intensity-dependent refractive index

sources: Hawking, Communications in Mathematical Physics 43, 199-220 (1975) — Full derivation
Bekenstein, Physical Review D 7, 2333 (1973) — BH entropy
Bardeen, Carter & Hawking, Communications in Mathematical Physics 31, 161 (1973) — Laws of BH mechanics
Page, Physical Review D 13, 198 (1976) — Particle emission rates
Page, PRL 71, 3743 (1993) — Page curve and information
Unruh, Physical Review D 14, 870 (1976) — Unruh effect
Almheiri, Marolf, Polchinski & Sully, JHEP 2013, 62 — Firewall
Maldacena & Susskind, Fortschritte der Physik 61, 781 (2013) — ER=EPR
Almheiri et al., JHEP 2020, 13 — Entropy of Hawking radiation
Muñoz de Nova et al., Nature 569, 688 (2019) — Analog Hawking radiation
Wald, 'Quantum Field Theory in Curved Spacetime and Black Hole Thermodynamics' (1994)
